title: Functions · 3 Types
scan: What you will learn: The three function types — Logical, Render, Feature/Helper — and the lifecycle helper pattern (initialize / go) that bridges them
Why it matters: Functions with no declared type get mixed purposes: a render function that also fetches, a helper that also mutates state — type discipline prevents this at the design stage
Logical functions must be pure — same input, same output, no side effects — if a "logical" function calls an API it is a feature function mislabelled
Lifecycle helpers are glue between framework events and business logic — keep them thin (≤10 lines) to make debugging fast

blocks.html: ost confusion in a codebase is one type of function quietly doing another's job. Keep them separate and the file almost organises itself.

Pure logical functions are the testability foundation. A function that only transforms data can be tested with zero mocking overhead — no server, no database, no DOM. This makes test authorship 10× faster and makes test output deterministic. Every pure logical function you extract from a mixed-purpose function is a test that writes itself.

Helper functions that accumulate business logic over time become the hidden controllers of an application. The warning sign: a helpers.js or utils.js file over 200 lines. Audit any helper file quarterly; migrate business logic to the correct function type.

cards.title: Logical
cards.body: Decide, compute, transform. They compute, invoke other code and call the API. No screen, no side-show — just answers.

cards.title: Render
cards.body: Take data and draw it — to the DOM, a canvas, a response body. They show; they don't decide.

cards.title: Feature / Helper
cards.body: Small reusable utilities the other two lean on. Format a date, validate a field, shape a payload.

items: initialize — set the stage: defaults, wiring, first state.
go / run — the entry point that kicks the whole thing into motion.

blocks.html: If a render function starts making decisions, or a logical function starts drawing — split it. That split is the cheapest refactor you'll ever do.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · Functions
items: Can each function be classified as logical / render / helper without ambiguity?
Are logical functions free of DOM manipulation?
Are render functions free of business decisions?
Is the initialize / go lifecycle consistent across features?
